examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1,2,3], 1:[0,4], 2:[0], 3:[0], 4:[1]}. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0, parent=-1. Visited={0}. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1 (parent 0). Visited={0,1}. From 1, visit neighbor 4 (parent 1). Visited={0,1,4}. Backtrack to 1, then 0. From 0, visit neighbor 2 (parent 0). Visited={0,1,4,2}. Backtrack. From 0, visit neighbor 3 (parent 0). Visited={0,1,4,2,3}. Step 4: No cycle detected (no visited neighbor except parent). Step 5: All 5 nodes visited, return true.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2,3], 2:[1,0], 3:[1]}. Step 2: DFS from node 0, parent=-1. Visited={0}. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1 (parent 0). Visited={0,1}. From 1, visit neighbor 2 (parent 1). Visited={0,1,2}. From 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent is 1), so cycle detected. Return false immediately.

approaches.name: DFS/BFS Traversal
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS or BFS traversal from any node to check for cycles and connectivity. If the graph has no cycles and all nodes are reachable from the start, it is a tree.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS/BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is a tree by checking connectivity and absence of cycles.
ladders.explanation: A tree is defined as a connected, acyclic graph. This problem requires verifying both properties. The intuition is that if we start a traversal from any node and can reach all nodes without encountering a cycle, the graph must be a tree. We use DFS or BFS to explore the graph. During traversal, we track visited nodes to ensure connectivity (all nodes visited) and check for cycles by ensuring we don't revisit a node except its immediate parent (in undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so we must avoid counting the back-edge to parent as a cycle). The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from edges. 2) Initialize visited set. 3) Perform DFS/BFS from node 0, passing parent to detect cycles. 4) If a cycle is detected, return false. 5) After traversal, check if all nodes are visited. If yes and no cycle, return true. Time: O(V+E) for traversal and adjacency list construction. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack/queue. Connection to L1: This integrates the cycle detection subroutine with connectivity check. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs, incorrectly detecting cycles by not tracking parent, forgetting to check all nodes visited. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, DFS with parent parameter, visited set, and post-traversal connectivity check.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1,2,3], 1:[0,4], 2:[0], 3:[0], 4:[1]}. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0, parent=-1. Visited={0}. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1 (parent 0). Visited={0,1}. From 1, visit neighbor 4 (parent 1). Visited={0,1,4}. Backtrack to 1, then 0. From 0, visit neighbor 2 (parent 0). Visited={0,1,4,2}. Backtrack. From 0, visit neighbor 3 (parent 0). Visited={0,1,4,2,3}. Step 4: No cycle detected (no visited neighbor except parent). Step 5: All 5 nodes visited, return true.

Step 1: Adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2,3], 2:[1,0], 3:[1]}. Step 2: DFS from node 0, parent=-1. Visited={0}. Step 3: From 0, visit neighbor 1 (parent 0). Visited={0,1}. From 1, visit neighbor 2 (parent 1). Visited={0,1,2}. From 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent is 1), so cycle detected. Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Connectivity Check
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to check if an undirected graph contains a cycle.
ladders.explanation: Cycle detection is crucial because a tree must be acyclic. The intuition: In an undirected graph, a cycle exists if during DFS, we encounter a visited node that is not the immediate parent of the current node. This works because DFS explores edges deeply; if we reach a node already visited via a different path, it indicates a back-edge forming a cycle. Algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list. 2) Use visited set. 3) DFS function takes current node and parent. 4) Mark current node visited. 5) For each neighbor, if not visited, recursively DFS with parent=current. If visited and neighbor != parent, return true (cycle found). 6) If no cycle in all neighbors, return false. Time: O(V+E) as we traverse each node and edge once. Space: O(V) for visited and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called during the full traversal; if it returns true, the graph is not a tree. Common mistakes: Forgetting to pass parent, not handling disconnected graphs (this subroutine assumes connectivity; in L0, we call it from each component). Real code pattern: Recursive DFS with parent tracking and early exit on cycle detection.

examples.trace: Adjacency: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,0]}. DFS(0, parent=-1): visited={0}. Neighbor 1: not visited, DFS(1, parent=0): visited={0,1}. Neighbor 0: visited and 0 != parent (parent is 0? Wait, parent for node 1 is 0, so neighbor 0 is parent, ignore). Neighbor 2: not visited, DFS(2, parent=1): visited={0,1,2}. Neighbor 1: visited and 1 != parent (parent is 1? Parent for node 2 is 1, so neighbor 1 is parent, ignore). Neighbor 0: visited and 0 != parent (parent is 1), so cycle detected, return true.

Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. DFS(0, parent=-1): visited={0}. Neighbor 1: not visited, DFS(1, parent=0): visited={0,1}. Neighbor 0: visited but is parent, ignore. Neighbor 2: not visited, DFS(2, parent=1): visited={0,1,2}. Neighbor 1: visited but is parent, ignore. Neighbor 3: not visited, DFS(3, parent=2): visited={0,1,2,3}. Neighbor 2: visited but is parent, ignore. No cycle, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Acyclic + Connected
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on a graph while tracking the parent node to avoid false cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for cycle detection in undirected graphs is to distinguish between revisiting a parent (which is normal in DFS) and revisiting a non-parent node (indicating a cycle). Parent tracking ensures we don't mistake the bidirectional edge to the parent as a cycle. Intuition: In DFS, we explore from a node to its neighbors; the node we came from is the parent. Since edges are undirected, the adjacency list includes the parent as a neighbor, so we must skip it. Algorithm: 1) Start DFS at a node with parent = -1 (or null). 2) Mark node as visited. 3) For each neighbor: if not visited, recursively DFS with parent = current node. If visited and neighbor != parent, cycle detected. 4) Continue until all reachable nodes are processed. Time: O(V+E) as we visit each node and edge. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is the heart of the cycle detection subroutine. Common mistakes: Not initializing parent correctly for the start node, incorrectly comparing parent. Real code pattern: Recursive function dfs(node, parent) that iterates over adjacency list and checks visited status with parent condition.

examples.trace: Adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. DFS(0, -1): visited={0}. Neighbor 1: not visited, call DFS(1, 0): visited={0,1}. Neighbor 0: visited and 0 == parent (parent is 0), so skip. Neighbor 2: not visited, call DFS(2, 1): visited={0,1,2}. Neighbor 1: visited and 1 == parent, skip. Return no cycle.

Adjacency: {0:[1,2], 1:[0,2], 2:[0,1]}. DFS(0, -1): visited={0}. Neighbor 1: not visited, call DFS(1, 0): visited={0,1}. Neighbor 0: visited and 0 == parent, skip. Neighbor 2: not visited, call DFS(2, 1): visited={0,1,2}. Neighbor 0: visited and 0 != parent (parent is 1), so cycle detected, return true.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency list representation for efficient traversal.
ladders.explanation: To traverse a graph efficiently, we need a data structure that allows quick access to a node's neighbors. An adjacency list is ideal for DFS/BFS as it provides O(1) average time to retrieve neighbors and uses O(V+E) space. Intuition: Represent the graph as a dictionary or list of lists where index i holds the list of nodes adjacent to node i. For undirected graphs, each edge adds two entries. Algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list of size V, each element an empty list. 2) For each edge [u,v]: append v to list[u] and u to list[v]. 3) Return the adjacency list. Time: O(E) to process all edges. Space: O(V+E) for the list structure. Connection to L2: This adjacency list is used in DFS traversal to iterate over neighbors. Common mistakes: Not initializing the list properly for all nodes, forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, off-by-one errors if nodes are 0-indexed. Real code pattern: Use a list of lists in Python: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversal / Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Perform a depth-first search traversal on a graph using a stack.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to mimic recursion using an explicit stack, which is useful to avoid recursion depth limits. Algorithm: 1) Start with a stack containing the start node and a visited set. 2) While stack is not empty: pop a node, if not visited, mark visited and push all unvisited neighbors onto stack. 3) Continue until stack is empty. This visits all nodes reachable from the start. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space: O(V) for stack and visited set. Connection to L3: This traversal uses the adjacency list built earlier to access neighbors. Common mistakes: Not marking visited when popping (can cause infinite loops), pushing neighbors without checking visited status. Real code pattern: Use a list as stack in Python: stack = [start]; visited = set(); while stack: node = stack.pop(); if node not in visited: visited.add(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: stack=[0], visited={}. Step 2: pop 0, not visited, visited={0}, push neighbor 1 -> stack=[1]. Step 3: pop 1, not visited, visited={0,1}, push neighbors 0 and 2: 0 is visited, so push 2 -> stack=[2]. Step 4: pop 2, not visited, visited={0,1,2}, push neighbor 1 (visited, skip). Stack empty, done.

Step 1: stack=[0], visited={}. Step 2: pop 0, not visited, visited={0}, push neighbors 1 and 2 -> stack=[1,2]. Step 3: pop 2, not visited, visited={0,2}, push neighbors 0 and 1: 0 visited, push 1 -> stack=[1,1]. Step 4: pop 1 (top), not visited, visited={0,2,1}, push neighbors 0 and 2 (both visited, skip). Stack now [1], pop 1 (visited, skip). Stack empty, done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Trees & Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of trees, cycles, and connectivity in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a connected undirected graph with no cycles. It has exactly V-1 edges. To check if a graph is a tree: verify it is connected and acyclic (or has V-1 edges and is connected).

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: 0-1 and 1-2. Start at node 0: can reach 1 via edge 0-1, then 2 via edge 1-2. All nodes reachable, so graph is connected.
Nodes: 0,1,2,3. Edges: 0-1 and 2-3. Start at node 0: can reach 1, but cannot reach 2 or 3 because no edges connect the component {0,1} to {2,3}. Thus, graph is disconnected.

approaches.name: Union-Find (DSU)
approaches.summary: Use a union-find data structure to process edges and detect cycles. If adding an edge connects two nodes already in the same set, a cycle exists, and the graph is not a tree.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find (DSU)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is a tree using Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a connected acyclic graph. Using Union-Find, we can efficiently check both properties simultaneously. The intuition is that as we process edges, if we ever find two nodes already in the same connected component, adding that edge would create a cycle, violating the tree property. Additionally, after processing all edges, we must verify the graph is connected (all nodes belong to the same set). The algorithm initializes a Union-Find structure for all nodes. For each edge (u, v), we check if find(u) == find(v). If yes, a cycle exists, return false. Otherwise, union(u, v). After processing edges, check if all nodes have the same root (connected). Time complexity is O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (practically constant). Space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This approach efficiently handles both cycle detection and connectivity check in one pass. Common mistakes include not checking connectivity after processing edges, or incorrectly handling self-loops (which immediately create cycles). The algorithm naturally handles parallel edges since they'll be detected as cycles when processed.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [0,2]: find(0)=0, find(2)=2, different → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [0,3]: find(0)=0, find(3)=3, different → union(0,3): parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,4]: find(1)=0 (path compressed), find(4)=4, different → union(0,4): parent[4]=0, rank[0]=1. All edges processed, no cycles. Check connectivity: all nodes have root 0 → connected. Return true.

Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, different → union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same root → cycle detected! Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Connectivity Check
ladders.desc: Implement the Union-Find data structure with optimizations for efficient operations.
ladders.explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) maintains a collection of disjoint sets with two primary operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). The intuition is to represent each set as a tree where each node points to its parent, with the root representing the set identifier. Path compression flattens the tree during find operations by making nodes point directly to the root, reducing future find times. Union by rank attaches the shorter tree under the taller one to keep trees balanced. These optimizations achieve near-constant time per operation (inverse Ackermann function). The algorithm maintains parent array (initially each node is its own parent) and rank array (initial height 0). Find(x) recursively finds root while compressing paths. Union(x,y) finds roots of x and y, if different, attaches lower rank root under higher rank root (if equal ranks, increment one rank). Time: O(α(n)) per operation. Space: O(n). This sub-routine is crucial for the full problem as it enables efficient cycle detection and connectivity checking. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly (must update parent during recursion) or confusing rank with size. The optimized structure ensures the graph algorithm runs in O(E * α(V)) time.

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2), find(4)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0 → attach 1 under 0, parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank[2]=0, rank[3]=0 → attach 3 under 2, parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1 → attach 2 under 0, parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. find(4): returns 4. Final parent: after path compression during finds, parent=[0,0,0,2,4].

examples.input: n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(3)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0 → attach 1 under 0, parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank[2]=0, rank[3]=0 → attach 3 under 2, parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. find(0): returns 0. find(3): recursively find parent 2, then 2 is root, compress path: parent[3]=2, returns 2.

testCases.input: n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(1), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(4)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(5)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), find(2), find(4)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(0,2), union(0,3), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), union(0,4), find(4)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Acyclic + Connected
ladders.desc: Detect if adding an edge creates a cycle by checking if endpoints are already connected.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for tree checking is that a tree has no cycles. Using Union-Find, we can detect cycles efficiently as we build the graph. The intuition: initially, each node is in its own set. When we add an edge between two nodes, we check if they're already in the same connected component (same set). If they are, adding this edge would create a cycle because there's already a path between them. If not, we merge their sets. This process naturally builds connected components. For an undirected graph to be a tree, we need exactly V-1 edges and no cycles. The Union-Find approach checks the no-cycle condition incrementally. The algorithm processes edges one by one: for edge (u,v), if find(u) == find(v), return false (cycle detected). Otherwise, union(u,v). After processing all edges, we still need to check connectivity separately. Time per edge is O(α(V)). This logic is the heart of the full solution. Common mistakes include not handling the case where multiple edges connect the same nodes (parallel edges), which Union-Find correctly identifies as cycles. Another mistake is forgetting that self-loops (edge from node to itself) always create cycles. The beauty is that this method detects cycles early without needing full graph traversal.

examples.trace: Initialize sets: {0},{1},{2},{3}. Edge [0,1]: find(0)≠find(1) → union, sets: {0,1},{2},{3}. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union, sets: {0,1,2},{3}. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, different → union, sets: {0,1,2,3}. All edges processed, no cycle detected.

Initialize sets: {0},{1},{2}. Edge [0,1]: find(0)≠find(1) → union, sets: {0,1},{2}. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, different → union, sets: {0,1,2}. Edge [2,0]: find(2)=0, find(0)=0, same root → cycle detected! Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Determine if all nodes in a graph are connected (single connected component).
ladders.explanation: A tree must be connected (all nodes reachable from each other). After ensuring no cycles, we must verify connectivity. The intuition: if the graph is connected, there exists a path between any two nodes. Using Union-Find, after processing all edges, all nodes should belong to the same set. If there are multiple sets, the graph is disconnected. The algorithm: after building Union-Find with all edges, iterate through all nodes, find their roots. If all roots are identical, the graph is connected. Alternatively, count distinct roots; if count > 1, disconnected. Time: O(V * α(V)). Space: O(1) additional. This building block is essential because a graph could be acyclic but disconnected (a forest), which isn't a tree. Common mistakes include not checking connectivity after cycle detection, or incorrectly assuming that having V-1 edges guarantees connectivity (it doesn't without the cycle check). Another mistake is checking connectivity by seeing if number of edges equals V-1, but that's necessary not sufficient. The Union-Find approach elegantly handles both checks: cycle detection during edge processing, connectivity check afterward.

examples.trace: Process edges with Union-Find: after unions, all nodes will be in same set. Check roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=0, find(3)=0. All same root → connected.
Process edges: union(0,1) → set {0,1}. union(2,3) → set {2,3}. Check roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=2, find(3)=2. Two distinct roots (0 and 2) → disconnected.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversal / Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation that locates the root of a set while compressing paths.
ladders.explanation: The find operation determines which set an element belongs to by finding the root of its tree. Path compression optimizes future finds by flattening the tree: as we recursively find the root, we update each node's parent to point directly to the root. The intuition: without compression, trees can become tall, making finds O(n). Compression ensures near-constant time. The algorithm: if parent[x] != x, then parent[x] = find(parent[x]) (recursive compression), return parent[x]. This recursively climbs to root while updating ancestors. Time: O(α(n)) amortized. Space: O(n) for recursion stack (can be iterative). This operation is fundamental to Union-Find efficiency. Common mistakes include not updating parent during recursion (missing compression), or using iterative approach without proper compression. Another mistake is confusing find with getting immediate parent instead of root. The recursive implementation clearly shows compression: each call updates the parent pointer. This operation feeds into union and connectivity checks.

examples.trace: Initial parent: 3→2, 2→1, 1→0, 0→0. find(3): parent[3]=2 ≠ 3 → call find(2). find(2): parent[2]=1 ≠ 2 → call find(1). find(1): parent[1]=0 ≠ 1 → call find(0). find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=0, return 0. Back to find(2): set parent[2]=0, return 0. Back to find(3): set parent[3]=0, return 0. Final parent: all point to 0.
find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. No compression needed.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Trees & Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of trees, cycles, and connectivity in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a connected undirected graph with no cycles. It has exactly V-1 edges. To check if a graph is a tree: verify it is connected and acyclic (or has V-1 edges and is connected).

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2]. union(0,1): find root of 0 is 0, root of 1 is 1, set parent[1]=0 → parent=[0,0,2]. union(1,2): find root of 1 is 0 (since parent[1]=0), root of 2 is 2, set parent[2]=0 → parent=[0,0,0]. Now all nodes have root 0.
Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3]. union(0,1): roots 0 and 1, set parent[1]=0 → parent=[0,0,2,3]. union(2,3): roots 2 and 3, set parent[3]=2 → parent=[0,0,2,2]. Two sets: {0,1} and {2,3}.

approaches.name: Graph Properties Verification
approaches.summary: Check if the graph satisfies tree properties: it must be connected and have exactly V-1 edges. First verify the edge count, then use DFS/BFS to confirm connectivity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Graph Properties Verification
ladders.desc: Complete solution verifying graph is connected and has exactly V-1 edges
ladders.explanation: This solution checks if a given undirected graph is a tree by verifying two essential properties: (1) The graph must be connected (all vertices reachable from any starting vertex), and (2) It must have exactly V-1 edges, where V is the number of vertices. The intuition is that trees are minimally connected graphs - they have just enough edges to connect all vertices without creating cycles. First, we verify the edge count condition since it's O(1) and can quickly eliminate many non-trees. If E ≠ V-1, we immediately return false. If the edge count is correct, we perform BFS/DFS from vertex 0 to check connectivity. We maintain a visited set and traverse all reachable vertices. If we visit exactly V vertices, the graph is connected. The combination of these two checks guarantees the graph is a tree: V-1 edges ensures no cycles (since a connected graph with V vertices and V edges must have at least one cycle), and connectivity ensures all vertices are reachable. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the BFS/DFS traversal, with O(1) for edge check. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and queue/stack. Connection to real applications: This approach is used in network design to verify if a communication network forms a tree topology, which is efficient for broadcasting messages without loops. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle disconnected graphs with V-1 edges (which would incorrectly return true), not using visited tracking in BFS/DFS (causing infinite loops), and not handling empty graphs or single vertex cases properly. The edge count check should come first for optimization since it's a quick filter.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check edge count. V=5, E=4. Since E=V-1 (4=5-1), proceed. Step 2: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3]}. Step 3: BFS from vertex 0. Initialize visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 4: Pop 0, mark visited={0}, add neighbors: queue=[1]. Step 5: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 2: queue=[2]. Step 6: Pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, add neighbor 3: queue=[3]. Step 7: Pop 3, visited={0,1,2,3}, add neighbor 4: queue=[4]. Step 8: Pop 4, visited={0,1,2,3,4}. Step 9: Check visited size=5 equals V=5, so connected. Step 10: Both conditions satisfied, return true.
Step 1: Check edge count. V=4, E=4. Since E≠V-1 (4≠3), immediately return false. The graph has a cycle (0-1-2-0) and too many edges, so it cannot be a tree.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Cycle Detection / Connectivity Check
ladders.desc: Check if all vertices in an undirected graph are reachable from a starting vertex
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine determines whether an undirected graph is connected by performing Breadth-First Search (BFS) from a given starting vertex (typically vertex 0). The intuition is that in a connected graph, starting from any vertex, you should be able to reach every other vertex through some path. BFS systematically explores vertices level by level, using a queue to manage the frontier. We maintain a visited set to track which vertices have been processed. The algorithm starts by marking the starting vertex as visited and adding it to the queue. While the queue is not empty, we dequeue a vertex, examine all its neighbors, and for each unvisited neighbor, mark it as visited and enqueue it. After BFS completes, we check if the number of visited vertices equals the total number of vertices V. If yes, the graph is connected; otherwise, it's disconnected. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed at most once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited set and queue. This connects to L0 by providing the connectivity verification component - after confirming the edge count condition, we use this BFS routine to check if the graph is connected. Common mistakes include not using visited tracking (leading to infinite loops), forgetting to handle empty graphs, and incorrectly assuming the graph is directed. The BFS approach is preferred over DFS for this task because it uses less stack space and is easier to implement iteratively, though both work equally well. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list representation, a set for visited, and a deque for the queue.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0. visited=set(), queue=[0]. Step 2: Pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbors 1,2: queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 0 (already visited): queue=[2]. Step 4: Pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, add neighbors 0 (visited), 3: queue=[3]. Step 5: Pop 3, visited={0,1,2,3}. Step 6: BFS ends. visited size=4 equals V=4, so connected.

Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0. visited=set(), queue=[0]. Step 2: Pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbor 1: queue=[1]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 0 (visited): queue=[]. Step 4: BFS ends. visited size=2, but V=5, so disconnected.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Acyclic + Connected
ladders.desc: Understand why connected graph with V-1 edges must be a tree
ladders.explanation: This core insight establishes that for an undirected graph G with V vertices, G is a tree if and only if it is connected and has exactly V-1 edges. The intuition comes from graph theory: A tree is defined as a connected acyclic graph. The key is that connectivity ensures all vertices are reachable, while the edge count condition ensures no cycles. Why does V-1 edges guarantee no cycles in a connected graph? Consider: A connected graph with V vertices must have at least V-1 edges (a spanning tree). If it has exactly V-1 edges, it's minimally connected - removing any edge would disconnect it. If it had V or more edges, by the pigeonhole principle, there must be at least one cycle (since a connected graph with V vertices can have at most V-1 edges without cycles). Conversely, if a graph is connected and acyclic, it must have exactly V-1 edges. This gives us an efficient two-step verification: First check E = V-1 (O(1)), then check connectivity via BFS/DFS (O(V+E)). This is more efficient than cycle detection algorithms. The connection to L1 is that once we confirm the edge count, we use connectivity check (BFS) to complete the verification. Common mistakes include not understanding why both conditions are necessary (e.g., a graph with V-1 edges but disconnected is not a tree), or confusing directed vs undirected graphs (this applies to undirected trees). This property is fundamental in network design, database indexing (B-trees), and hierarchical data structures.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if connected: yes. Step 2: Check if E=V-1: 3=4-1, yes. Step 3: Both conditions satisfied, so it's a tree. Example: edges (0-1, 1-2, 2-3) forms a path graph which is a tree.
Step 1: Check if connected: no (e.g., edges (0-1, 2-3) gives two disconnected components). Step 2: Even though E=V-1 (3=3), the graph is disconnected, so it's not a tree. A tree must be connected.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Convert edge list to adjacency list for efficient graph traversal
ladders.explanation: This building block involves converting a graph given as a list of edges into an adjacency list representation, which is crucial for efficient BFS/DFS traversal. The intuition is that adjacency lists provide O(1) access to a vertex's neighbors and are space-efficient for sparse graphs (typical for trees). Given V vertices and a list of edges (each edge [u,v] representing an undirected connection), we create an array or dictionary of lists where index i contains all vertices adjacent to vertex i. For undirected graphs, each edge [u,v] adds v to u's list and u to v's list. The algorithm: Initialize an empty adjacency list with V empty lists. For each edge [u,v], append v to adj[u] and append u to adj[v]. Time complexity is O(E) to process all edges. Space complexity is O(V+E) to store the adjacency lists. This connects to L2 because once we have the adjacency list, we can efficiently perform BFS to check connectivity. Common mistakes include forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, using incorrect data structures (like adjacency matrix which is O(V²) space), or not handling duplicate edges (trees shouldn't have them, but the representation should be robust). The adjacency list is preferred over edge list for traversal because it allows immediate access to neighbors without scanning all edges. Real code pattern: Use list of lists in Python, ensuring to initialize with V empty lists.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[],[]] for V=4. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0]=[1], add 0 to adj[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: add 2 to adj[1]=[0,2], add 1 to adj[2]=[1]. Step 4: Process edge [2,3]: add 3 to adj[2]=[1,3], add 2 to adj[3]=[2]. Final: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1,3], adj[3]=[2].

Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: add 1 to adj[0]=[1], add 0 to adj[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process edge [0,2]: add 2 to adj[0]=[1,2], add 0 to adj[2]=[0]. Final: adj[0]=[1,2], adj[1]=[0], adj[2]=[0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversal / Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Visit all vertices in a graph level by level using a queue
ladders.explanation: BFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices in layers based on distance from a starting vertex. The intuition is to visit all neighbors of the current vertex before moving to their neighbors, mimicking a wavefront expansion. It uses a queue (First-In-First-Out) to manage the order of exploration. The algorithm: Start with a visited set and a queue containing the starting vertex. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a vertex, mark it as visited, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This ensures vertices are visited in increasing order of distance from the start. For connectivity checking, we don't need to track distances, just visited vertices. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex is enqueued/dequeued once and each edge is examined once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and visited set. This connects to L3 because BFS operates on the adjacency list representation to traverse the graph. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (causing duplicates), using a stack instead of queue (which would be DFS), and not handling empty graphs. BFS is iterative and avoids recursion depth issues, making it suitable for large graphs. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for efficient queue operations in Python.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=set(), queue=deque([0]). Step 2: pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbors 1,2: queue=[1,2]. Step 3: pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbors 0(visited),3: queue=[2,3]. Step 4: pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, add neighbor 0(visited): queue=[3]. Step 5: pop 3, visited={0,1,2,3}, add neighbor 1(visited): queue=[]. BFS ends.

Step 1: visited=set(), queue=deque([0]). Step 2: pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbor 1: queue=[1]. Step 3: pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbors 0(visited),2: queue=[2]. Step 4: pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, add neighbors 1(visited),3: queue=[3]. Step 5: pop 3, visited={0,1,2,3}, add neighbor 2(visited): queue=[]. BFS ends.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Trees & Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of trees, cycles, and connectivity in undirected graphs.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a connected undirected graph with no cycles. It has exactly V-1 edges. To check if a graph is a tree: verify it is connected and acyclic (or has V-1 edges and is connected).

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0,1,2 → V=3. Step 2: Count edges: [0,1] is one edge, [1,2] is another edge → E=2. Note: Each edge connects two vertices.
Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2,3 → V=4. Step 2: Edges: [0,1], [1,2], [2,3], [3,0] → four distinct edges → E=4.
